task: I got a notice saying a chargeback or complaint is being reviewed, and I want to handle it the right way without making a mess of it. What should I save, who should I contact first, and what usually needs to be followed up in writing?

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the clean way to handle it:

1. **Freeze the situation**
   - Don’t argue by phone.
   - Don’t admit the charge is valid.
   - Don’t make side payments unless you’ve decided that separately.

2. **Save everything**
   - The notice itself
   - The bill/invoice
   - The contract or terms
   - Emails, texts, portal messages
   - Call logs: date, time, who you spoke to, and what was said
   - Screenshots of any account activity or deadlines

3. **Contact the right party first**
   - If this is a **card chargeback**, contact the **card issuer or bank** first and ask what they need from you and the deadline.
   - If this is a **company complaint or billing dispute**, contact the **company’s billing or dispute department** first.
   - If you already sent a complaint, follow up with the same place in writing.

4. **Get key points in writing**
   Ask for:
   - The exact charge being reviewed
   - The basis for the charge
   - Any documents they relied on
   - The timeline for review
   - Whether anything is paused while the review is pending

5. **Follow up in writing whenever it matters**
   Put in writing:
   - Your dispute
   - Any request for the basis of the charge
   - Any correction you want
   - Any promise they make on the phone

6. **Don’t let it drift**
   - Track deadlines carefully.
   - If they’re threatening collections, shutoff, lien, or saying the contract controls against you, that’s the point to consider legal help.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist** or a **short dispute email** you can send today.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d want it tighter, and with one thing clearer: if there’s an actual deadline on the notice, that gets saved and tracked first. I’m not relying on somebody’s phone promise.

What I’d keep:
- the notice, front and back if there is anything on both sides
- the invoice or statement
- the contract, estimate, terms, receipt
- any emails, texts, portal messages
- names, dates, times, and what was said on calls
- screenshots if there’s an account page, status, or deadline
- proof I sent anything, if I mail or upload it

Who I’d contact first depends on who opened the review:
- bank/card issuer if it’s a chargeback
- the company’s billing or dispute office if it’s their complaint process

What needs to be in writing:
- my dispute, plainly stated
- any request for the basis of the charge
- any response or correction they claim to make
- any deadline extension or hold on collections/shutoff if they say that over the phone

I’d also want to know what not to say in the response. People talk too much on the phone and hand over exactly what the other side wants.

turns.agent_response:
You’re right. Keep it tight and treat the deadline as the first thing to preserve.

## What to save first
- The notice, **front and back**
- **Any deadline** on it, plus the date you received it
- Invoice or statement
- Contract, estimate, terms, receipt
- Emails, texts, portal messages
- Call log: **name, date, time, what was said**
- Screenshots of account status, review status, deadlines
- Proof of anything you send: certified mail receipt, upload confirmation, email sent copy

## Who to contact first
- **Chargeback / card dispute:** the **bank or card issuer**
- **Company complaint / billing dispute:** the **company’s billing or dispute department**

If you’re unsure which process it is, go by **who opened the review** and **what the notice says**.

## What should be in writing
Put these in writing:
- Your dispute, plainly and briefly
- A request for the **basis of the charge**
- Any correction they say they made
- Any deadline extension, hold, or pause on collections/shutoff if they mention it
- Anything important they say by phone

## What not to say on the phone
Don’t:
- Admit the charge is valid
- Explain too much or speculate
- Guess at dates, amounts, or terms
- Agree to a payment plan unless you mean to
- Say you “understand” or “accept” the charge if you don’t
- Waive anything, even casually
- Rely on “we’ll note it in the system” without written confirmation

## Safe phone script
Use something like:
> “I’m requesting the basis for the charge and the current status of the review. Please send that in writing. I’m not agreeing to anything by phone.”

## When to be more careful
If they mention:
- collections
- shutoff
- lien
- arbitration
- waiver of rights
- contract terms being used against you

that’s when it stops being just a complaint and starts looking like something to take more seriously.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-paragraph phone script** and a **short follow-up email** you can send right away.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still want one more layer: the exact deadline and where it came from. If it’s on the notice, that goes first. If they gave it by phone, I’d treat it as unhelpful until it’s in writing.

And I’d want the response itself pared down even more. No story. No argument. Just:
- I dispute the charge/reviewed item
- the reason, in one sentence if needed
- the account number / reference number
- the deadline I’m relying on
- a request for written confirmation of status

What not to say matters, yes, but I’d like a cleaner list of the traps:
- don’t explain your whole history
- don’t guess at anything
- don’t offer partial blame just to sound reasonable
- don’t agree the record is correct if it isn’t
- don’t ask for “a call back” and leave it there

And one more thing: if there’s any chance this turns into collections or shutoff, I’d want the writing to go out the same day, not “when I get around to it.” That kind of delay bites people.

So, tighter version, then maybe the one-paragraph script.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter version.

## Save first
- The notice, **front and back**
- The **exact deadline** on the notice, and **where it came from**
- If the deadline was only said by phone, treat it as **not confirmed** until it’s in writing
- Invoice or statement
- Contract, estimate, terms, receipt
- Emails, texts, portal messages
- Call log: name, date, time, what was said
- Screenshots of account status, review status, deadlines
- Proof of anything you send: mail receipt, upload confirmation, sent email

## Who to contact first
- **Chargeback / card dispute:** the **bank or card issuer**
- **Company complaint / billing dispute:** the **company’s billing or dispute office**

If the notice says who opened the review, follow that process first.

## What needs to be in writing
- Your dispute, plainly stated
- The **reason** for the charge, if you’re asking for it
- The **account number / reference number**
- The **deadline** you’re relying on
- Any response, correction, extension, hold, or pause they claim
- Anything important said by phone

## What not to say
- Don’t tell your whole story
- Don’t guess at dates, amounts, or terms
- Don’t offer partial blame just to sound reasonable
- Don’t agree the record is correct if it isn’t
- Don’t rely on “call me back” without written follow-up

## If collections or shutoff is possible
Send the writing **the same day**. Don’t wait.

## Short response script
> I dispute the charge/reviewed item.  
> Reference/account number: [number].  
> The deadline I’m relying on is [date], from [notice / written notice].  
> Please confirm the current status in writing and provide the basis for the charge/reviewed item.

If you want, I can turn that into a **very short email or portal message** you can paste in as-is.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the shape of it. Tight enough.

I’d keep the deadline and source of it right at the top, exactly. If it’s only a phone deadline, I’d treat it as noise until it’s written down.

The script is fine too. I’d probably make it even plainer when I use it, but this is usable:

> I dispute the charge/reviewed item. Reference/account number: [number]. The deadline I’m relying on is [date], from [notice / written notice]. Please confirm the current status in writing and provide the basis for the charge/reviewed item.

And yes — same day if collections or shutoff is even a possibility. No dawdling. That’s how people get boxed in.
